text:
The world is full of interesting things. Here is one: bees navigate using the position of the sun, but they can also detect polarized light through clouds. On an overcast day, the hive still knows which way is which.

Here is another: the number zero was controversial. For centuries, serious thinkers resisted it. A number that meant nothing, that stood for absence, seemed suspicious, almost dangerous. Now it is so obvious that children learn it before they learn to read.

Language works the same way. Words we use every day were once strange imports, borrowed words that felt foreign on the tongue. Algebra, coffee, magazine, admiral. These all came from Arabic. The borrowing happened slowly, then completely, until no one could imagine the language without them.

There is a kind of satisfaction in small precise things. The exact weight of a letter. The specific sound a lock makes when it closes correctly. The way a well-made chair does not wobble. These things are not beautiful in any showy way. They are just right, and rightness has its own feeling.

Mountains are older than almost anything you can think of. Older than insects. Older than trees. The Appalachians were once as tall as the Himalayas are now. Rain and time took most of them. What remains is the root, the deep compressed rock, worn smooth. You walk on what was once buried under miles of stone.

People spend a lot of time worrying about what things mean. Sometimes things do not mean anything. A bird lands on a branch because it is a bird and there is a branch. The branch holds. The bird leaves. This is not a metaphor for anything.

But if you want a metaphor, here is a reasonable one: most problems are patient. They will wait. They do not need you to solve them immediately. Sometimes waiting is itself a kind of solution, because the problem changes shape, or you do.

That is enough for now. It was good to think about these things for a moment.
